title: Bounding Kirkwood-Dirac negativity of Gaussian processes
abstract: The Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobability provides an operational representation of a quantum state, whose negativity serves as a measure of nonclassicality. Despite its fundamental importance, the extremal values of the Kirkwood-Dirac negativity are still unknown in the general case. We investigate the Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobability of an arbitrary quantum state under Gaussian processes. In this setting, we derive an upper bound on the negativity for any number of modes and measurements. For a single mode and two measurements, we show that the eigenstates of the quadrature operators saturate this upper bound, while a nontrivial minimum is reached by pure Gaussian states. As a consequence, our results indicate that Gaussian states are sufficient to achieve extreme values of nonclassicality.

bite.hook: Gaussian states are mathematically proven to be sufficient for reaching extreme values of quantum nonclassicality.
tldr: They investigated the Kirkwood-Dirac (KD) quasiprobability negativity of arbitrary quantum states under Gaussian processes.
They derived an upper bound on this negativity for any number of modes and measurements.
They proved that quadrature operator eigenstates saturate the upper bound, while pure Gaussian states reach a nontrivial minimum.
bite.whyItMatters: Understanding the limits of quantum nonclassicality helps researchers identify which quantum states are most useful for quantum computing and sensing. This work proves that Gaussian states, which are relatively easy to create in labs, can reach the absolute limits of this quantum behavior.
bite.eli5: In the quantum world, some probabilities can behave as if they are negative, which is a key sign of 'quantum weirdness'. This study calculated the exact limits of this weirdness for a common class of quantum states called Gaussian states, proving they can reach the absolute maximum level of quantum behavior possible.

glossary.shortDef: A quantum representation that generalizes classical probability distributions but can take negative or complex values.
glossary.eli5Def: A special quantum scorecard that allows for 'negative probabilities' to describe quantum states.

glossary.shortDef: Physical processes or states characterized by Gaussian (bell-curve) probability distributions in their variables.
glossary.eli5Def: Physical systems whose properties naturally follow a standard, smooth bell-curve shape.

glossary.shortDef: Operators in quantum optics corresponding to the position and momentum-like coordinates of a light field.
glossary.eli5Def: The basic dials we turn to measure the position or momentum of a light wave.
